Full Length Article
A hybrid data envelopment analysis and artificial intelligence framework 
for sustainable supplier selection: a case study in the 
petrochemical industry
Maedeh GholamAzad
, Alireza Eydi *
Department of Industrial Engineering, University of Kurdistan, Sanandaj, Iran
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Supply Chain
Sustainable Supplier
Most Productive Scale Size
Artificial Neural Networks
K-Nearest Neighbors
Chi-Squared Automatic Interaction Detection
Petrochemical Industry
A B S T R A C T
As global industrial operations expand, the complexity and volume of suppliers have increased, making sus­
tainable supplier selection (SSS) a strategic imperative for resilient supply chains (SCs). Traditional evaluation 
methods often fail to handle large datasets and dynamic sustainability metrics, resulting in suboptimal decisions. 
This study introduces a novel hybrid intelligent framework that integrates Most Productive Scale Size Data 
Envelopment Analysis (MPSS-DEA) with three Artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms—Artificial Neural Networks 
(ANNs), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Chi-squared Automatic Interaction Detection (CHAID)—to enhance 
both accuracy and scalability in supplier evaluation. Applied to 362 suppliers and 38 sustainability criteria in the 
petrochemical industry, the proposed framework achieved high classification accuracies of 92.5% (DEA-CHAID), 
91.9% (DEANN), and 91.4% (DEA-KNN). The model also demonstrated strong discrimination power, with ROC 
AUC scores of 0.96, 0.95, and 0.94, respectively. Predictor importance analysis revealed that some of the fea­
tures, such as R2, SE2, QA4, CA6, and DE3, were the most influential features across all models. Beyond per­
formance metrics, the framework offers real-time supplier replacement, reduced computational complexity, and 
modular adaptability across industries. It supports ethical and sustainable sourcing by integrating economic, 
environmental, and social dimensions into decision-making. The intelligent architecture enables lifecycle anal­
ysis, promotes transparency, and aligns with global sustainability standards. This research contributes a scalable, 
interpretable, and data-driven solution for sustainable supplier selection, bridging the gap between traditional 
DEA models and modern artificial intelligence (AI) techniques. Its applicability across diverse industrial contexts 
positions it as a robust tool for strategic procurement and supply chain resilience.
1. Introduction
Fig. 1 presents the graphical abstract of the proposed hybrid intel­
ligent framework for SSS in the petrochemical industry. It visualizes the 
integration of MPSS-DEA with three AI algorithms—ANN, KNN, and 
CHAID—alongside comparative performance metrics. Among the 
models, DEA-CHAID achieved the highest accuracy (92.5 %), under­
scoring the framework’s robustness in managing large-scale datasets 
and complex sustainability criteria.
To enhance clarity and accessibility for readers unfamiliar with 
technical abbreviations and domain-specific terms, Table 1 provides a 
comprehensive nomenclature. It summarizes all key symbols, acronyms, 
and variables used throughout the manuscript, particularly those related 
to DEA modeling, ML algorithms, and supplier evaluation metrics.
As environmental concerns and corporate social responsibility 
continue to rise globally, organizations are under increasing pressure to 
adopt sustainable practices throughout their SCs[23]. Procurement 
plays a pivotal role in maintaining SC competitiveness by ensuring 
timely delivery and high-quality products. A critical component of 
procurement is supplier selection (SS), which directly influences oper­
ational success and long-term strategic outcomes. Poor supplier choices 
can lead to delays, financial losses, and reputational damage[32].
SSS has emerged as a key process for evaluating suppliers based on 
environmental, social, and economic criteria. However, traditional 
evaluation methods often rely on subjective judgments and limited data, 
rendering them inadequate for today’s data-intensive decision-making 
environments [37]. As industries face increasingly complex SCs and 
large volumes of supplier data, the need for advanced analytical tools

minimize the limitations associated with individual methods. Before 
delving into the literature review, Table 2 presents some of the key 
criteria frequently used in various studies.
2.1. MCDM methods for supplier selection
Identifying and evaluating appropriate suppliers is crucial to the 
success of any organization. The SS process involves several key objec­
tives, such as minimizing purchasing risk, ensuring customer satisfac­
tion, and fostering long-term relationships between buyers and 
suppliers. Various methods-including AHP, Analytic Network Process 
(ANP), DEA, TOPSIS, and ELECTRE- are utilized to assess suppliers and 
identify the best options[56]. Bafrooei et al. [11] proposed a combined 
DEA-MCDM model to rank suppliers in Iran’s petrochemical industry. 
They introduced a new criterion, “executive capability,” based on their 
own experiments and expert opinions. The findings revealed the sig­
nificant importance of this criterion in SS within the petrochemical 
sector. Sayfi and Nikbakht [51] proposed an integrated approach to 
identify and prioritize criteria for selecting green suppliers in the 
petrochemical industry. They used a T-test to identify relevant criteria 
and employed the FANP method for supplier ranking.
Prasad and Kambagowni [46] proposed a combined approach using 
DEA, AHP, and GRA to evaluate and select the best suppliers for com­
panies. They applied this method in a steel plant in southern India, 
demonstrating its effectiveness for both qualitative and quantitative 
criteria. He and Zhang [26] introduced an integrated framework based 
on factor analysis (FA), DEA, and AHP to select suitable suppliers for 
cement firms. The results indicated that the proposed method could 
comprehensively evaluate and rank efficient suppliers. Zarbakhshnia 
and Jamali Jaghdani [64] and Eydi and GholamAzad [20] developed a 
novel NDEA and NEREC- NDEA models that considers uncontrollable 
and undesirable outputs for SS in Iran’s plastic industry. Their findings 
showed that the model possesses reliable and valid characteristics, 
making it suitable for application in similar industries. Tong et al. [57] 
presented a valuable approach using FTOPSIS for assessing equipment 
maintenance vendors in the chemical sector. They employed eight pri­
mary criteria and twenty-four sub-criteria to select appropriate sup­
pliers, ensuring future operational safety and sustainability. The 
findings demonstrated the method’s significant practical utility for 
adoption in chemical industries. Wongthong and Paoprasert [60] 
introduced a novel SS approach for maintenance tasks in Thailand’s 
petrochemical sector, utilizing confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and 
structural equation modeling (SEM). Their findings indicated that fac­
tors such as cost, quality, flexibility, and human resources greatly in­
fluence SS. Tong et al. [58] proposed a new framework for selecting and 
evaluating suitable suppliers for a Chinese petrochemical company 
using the fuzzy PROMETHEE II method. This approach was structured 
around three dimensions: fundamental, procedural, and sustainability 
factors. The results highlighted the method’s efficacy in SS.
Karami et al. [33] proposed a three-stage hybrid method- PCA-DEA- 
VIKOR- to address SS issues in garment SCs. The study demonstrated the 
effectiveness of this integrated approach for application in similar SCs.

5:
SS and evaluation in the circular SC. Their study focused on six petro­
chemical companies in Iran, showing that the proposed framework of­
fers greater efficiency and applicability compared to existing methods. 
Nguyen et al. [42] suggested a new integrated method based on DEA-SF- 
AHP and SF-WASPAS to identify and select sustainable suppliers in 
Vietnam’s steel manufacturing sector. The findings indicated that the 
proposed integrated method is a practical and robust approach to SS and 
can be effectively utilized in the context of big data for the steel industry.
Gidiagba et al. [23] developed an integrated MCDM method for 
selecting suppliers in the oil and gas industry. They utilized the Best- 
Worst method (BWM) to determine the main criteria and ranked the 
suppliers using TOPSIS. Fifteen criteria were selected in this study, 
incorporating the Delphi technique. Nasri et al. [43] introduced an in­
tegrated FDEMATEL-ANP-DEA method to identify sustainable suppliers 
in Iranian petroleum companies. This method enables managers to 
reduce adverse environmental impacts and enhance the social aspects of 
the SC. Parsa Rad et al. [47] presented an integrated MCDM method 
using the fuzzy Best-Worst Method (FBWM) and the Gray Combined 
Compromise Solution (COCOSO) for SS in oil, gas, and petrochemical 
companies. The study aims to improve SS under uncertainty. The find­
ings indicate that the method expedites and streamlines the selection 
process in this industry.
2.2. AI methods for supplier selection
In recent years, numerous researchers have increasingly utilized ML 
and AI techniques to select sustainable suppliers. In this context, Omurca 
[45] introduced a novel approach using a fusion of fuzzy c-means (FCM) 
and rough set theory (RST) for SS and evaluation. The findings 
demonstrated that this method can identify top suppliers and categorize 
all vendors according to their fuzzy similarity levels. Guo et al. [21] 
proposed a new semi-fuzzy SVDD model to address the SS multi- 
classification issue. The study segmented observations into determin­
istic and fuzzy samples, enabling the method’s application under un­
certain conditions. This approach not only streamlines the calculation 
process but also enhances supplier performance. El-Hiri et al. [19] 
proposed an ANN-based method to reduce supply and demand risks, 
assisting manufacturers in selecting the most efficient suppliers. This 
approach enables managers to make appropriate supplier choices 
without relying on complex algorithms. Jafari Nodeh et al. [30] intro­
duced an intelligent supervised approach employing Multi-Layer Per­
ceptron (MLP), Decision Tree (DT), and K-means to select and evaluate 
suppliers in the oil, gas, and petrochemical sectors. This method reduces 
runtime and costs while effectively ranking top suppliers. Zhao et al. 
[65] presented a novel SVM-based approach to address SS challenges. 
Their findings indicated that the method is effective in categorizing 
expert data, assessing historical information, and assigning weights. Ali 
et al. [6] presented a random forest approach based on supervised ML 
for SS. This method can generate a detailed list of key criteria commonly 
used in supplier evaluation, allowing managers to focus on criteria most 
relevant to their suppliers. Abulouifa and Bahj [7] proposed an inte­
grated framework using ML algorithms such as AdaBoost, SVM, and 
KNN to select suppliers based on their orders and preferences. The 
findings revealed that the AdaBoost algorithm achieved the highest 
accuracy (98 %) compared to the others, with an F1-score of 86 %. 
Abdelhadi and Almomani [8] examined similarities among supplier 
groups using specific criteria. They introduced a novel clustering tech­
nique, Crisp Gradual Means Integral, to assess supplier similarity and 
assign each to a distinct category. The results indicated that this method 
can help managers avoid adverse situations by replacing unavailable 
suppliers with those possessing comparable attributes. Islam et al. [29] 
introduced a three-phase framework for addressing SS and order allo­
cation. By utilizing Seasonal Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average 
(SARIMA) and Light Gradient-Boosting Machine (LGBM), they predicted 
customer demand and compared the outcomes of both methods. Addi­
tionally, a deep network was established to recommend optimal choices 
for suppliers in the Canadian meat industry. The findings revealed sig­
nificant correlations among variables in SS. Mirzaee and Ashtab [41] 
conducted a comprehensive review of SS, focusing on sustainability, 
resiliency, and AI-based methods. Their study analyzed 143 research 
articles and covered various evaluation criteria, including economic, 
environmental, social, and resilience aspects. They explored multiple 
hybrid approaches, such as mathematical models, MCDM-ML tech­
niques, and AI algorithms. The study culminated in an integrated 
framework and identified seven potential directions for future research 
in this evolving field. Asghar et al. [9] employed an intelligent 
computing framework using the Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm (LMA) 
to train artificial neural networks for accurate prediction of fluid forces 
in transient non-Newtonian thermal flows. By integrating CFD- 
generated data with ANN-LMA, the model achieves high precision 
while significantly reducing computational cost. The approach offers a 
robust alternative to traditional simulations in complex engineering 
fluid dynamics. Yadav et al. [62] proposed an optimization method 
combining Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP) and ML to 
optimize digital procurement costs for SS, while ensuring transparency, 
traceability, security, and comprehensive information on a distributed 
ledger. This method is primarily applied to manage SCs for medical 
equipment, drugs, liquid oxygen, and other essential goods during epi­
demics. Shafiq Hira et al. [53] presented a computational framework 
combining CFD simulations with artificial neural networks (ANN) to 
analyze Darcy–Forchheimer flows in stenosed arteries. By modeling 
nonlinear fluid behavior in porous, constricted vessels, the ANN-CFD 
coupling enables accurate prediction of hemodynamic forces. The 
approach supports medical diagnostics and treatment planning by 
reducing simulation time while maintaining precision and accuracy. 
Gidiagba et al. [24] conducted a systematic review on the application of 
ML for SSS between 2010 and 2024. By analyzing 50 key studies from 
major databases, the authors demonstrated how ML techniques improve 
decision-making by managing complex, multi-dimensional data and 
addressing the shortcomings of traditional approaches. Their proposed 
framework aligns AI and conventional methods across all stages of 
supplier evaluation-criteria definition, weighting, assessment, and 
ranking- resulting in enhanced accuracy, efficiency, and organizational 
performance.
2.3. Methods of integrating MCDM and AI
Since each method for SS may have its own weaknesses, many re­
searchers have utilized a combination of various approaches to assess 
and select suppliers. For instance, Tavana et al. [54] introduced a novel 
ANN-based method and ANFIS to understand the relationships among 
key features in SS. In their study, the AHP method was employed to 
determine the most influential criteria for evaluating supplier perfor­
mance. The proposed method was tested in the automotive sector to 
demonstrate its robustness and responsiveness. Cheng et al. [17] pre­
sented an integrated DEA-Adaboost model for SS. Fuzzy multi-objective 
DEA was used to construct the expert dataset, and the Adaboost 
approach was applied to train the dataset for the intelligent model. The 
findings showed that the proposed model achieved higher accuracy 
compared to other models. Amindoust [1] proposed a resilient- 
sustainable method based on fuzzy set theory, fuzzy inference system 
(FIS), and AR-DEA to intelligently select suppliers. This integrated 
approach presents a comprehensive SS model with multiple criteria. The 
method was implemented in a case study of Imen Soukht Sepahan, and 
sensitivity analysis was conducted. Ijadi Maghsoodi et al. [28] intro­
duced a new method for SS using the MULTIMOORA technique and k- 
means cluster analysis, demonstrating a fast and efficient process based 
on distinct clusters. Abdulla et al. [2] proposed a hybrid MCDM-ML 
method utilizing AHP and DT for effective SS and evaluation. The 
method was applied to Libyan companies to highlight the DT’s lower 
complexity compared to AHP. Cheng et al. [18] presented an intelligent 
model for selecting and evaluating suppliers in global SCs using data-

6:
driven Support Vector Regression (SVR). In this context, the MCDM 
method was applied to determine supplier labels, and SVR was utilized 
for model training. The findings indicated that the proposed model 
demonstrated strong reliability and accuracy. Aggarwal et al. [5] pro­
posed a novel model combining DEA with SVM, DT, and logistic 
regression for supplier selection. The results identified DT as the most 
accurate method. Zhu et al. [66] introduced a novel approach inte­
grating the DEA method with ML algorithms to evaluate supplier effi­
ciency. They employed Back Propagation Neural Networks (BPNNs), 
ISVM, and Generative Adversarial Neural Network (GANN) algorithms 
alongside DEA in Chinese manufacturing firms. The findings showed a 
94 % average efficiency improvement using hybrid techniques, sur­
passing the performance of the standalone DEA model. Abdulla et al. [3] 
proposed a combined method based on the Measurement of Alternatives 
and Ranking according to Compromise Solution (MARCOS) and tree- 
based ML for evaluating and selecting sustainable suppliers. In their 
study, the importance of each criterion was determined using a decision 
tree and applied within the MARCOS method to rank suppliers in the oil 
and gas industry. They claimed that the proposed integrated method 
could be useful for designing an efficient and effective SS process. Liang 
et al. [35] introduced a hybrid framework combining MCDM and AI 
techniques to facilitate the effective integration of AI technology in SCM. 
They applied DEMATEL to identify relationships among criteria and 
used a clustering algorithm to classify automobile SC suppliers. The 
findings indicated the method’s applicability to other industries facing 
similar challenges. Ma and Li [40] proposed a conceptual framework 
consisting of three modules: the decision matrix and criteria initializa­
tion module, the MCDM method selection and implementation module, 
and the aggregation module. A key focus of their study is the develop­
ment of an intelligent core for decision support systems (DSSs) within 
the aggregation module, utilizing ML methods. This integrated approach 
not only evaluates supplier quality but also has broader applications. 
They tested their method in a large automotive company, demonstrating 
that the proposed DSS delivers more robust and reliable evaluation re­
sults compared to traditional MCDM methods. Abdulla and Baryannis 
[4] introduced a SS method that integrates interpretable, data-driven AI 
techniques with MCDM approaches. This study aims to simplify SS 
challenges while maintaining stakeholder familiarity by keeping MCDM 
central to the process. The method was validated through sensitivity 
analysis in oil, gas, and aerospace manufacturing companies, showing 
its capability and robustness compared to other methods that rely solely 
on ML techniques. Lin and Lu [36] developed a new analytical frame­
work that combines inverse DEA with ML to optimize the performance 
of Apple’s SC suppliers. Their study provided a systematic method to 
reduce CO2 emissions by 30 % to 50 % using Random Forest (RF) and 
KNN algorithms along with IDEA for evaluating supplier performance 
and identifying the most sustainable choices. The proposed hybrid 
method outlines a module for SC stakeholders to integrate sustainability 
into their operations. The results serve as guidance for CO2 reduction 
and help evaluate suppliers’ economic performance to achieve sustain­
ability goals. Nafei et al. [44] introduced a hybrid decision-making 
method combining the Technique for Order of Preference by Similar­
ity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) with Neuromorphic Triplets (NTs) to 
overcome the limitations of traditional methods in managing indeter­
minate information. This new approach enhances computational effi­
ciency and accuracy. The findings indicate that the intelligent TOPSIS 
method improves decision accuracy and simplifies computations 
compared to traditional TOPSIS. Furthermore, it can be adapted to 
various scenarios across different research fields and industries. 
Gidiagba et al. [25] proposed a hybrid supplier evaluation model for the 
pharmaceutical sector by integrating non-negative matrix factorization, 
random forest, and TOPSIS. It reduces 24 criteria to 8 core dimensions, 
assigns data-driven weights, and ranks suppliers effectively. Results 
show improved accuracy, interpretability, and sustainability compared 
to traditional MCDM approaches. Tajally et al. [59] proposed a novel 
hybrid ML-based model for SS by integrating sustainability, circular 
economy, and viability criteria. Using a case study in the automotive 
industry, it combines LGPSBWM for weighting dimensions and SWSVM 
for supplier classification. Key indicators such as cost, quality, and GHG 
emissions are identified, and the model selects Omani#01 and Ira­
nian#07 as top suppliers, demonstrating high efficiency and reliability.
Based on the reviewed literature and numerous studies conducted on 
SS across various industries- including petrochemicals- a comprehensive 
study involving multiple criteria and sub-criteria alongside a large 
number of suppliers specifically within the petrochemical sector has not 
been identified. Therefore, this study is inspired by prior research such 
as Bafrooei et al. [11], who applied the MCDM-DEA method for SS in an 
Iranian petrochemical firm; Wongthong and Paoprasert [60], who 
employed CFA and SEM for supplier evaluation in Thailand’s petro­
chemical industry; Nasri et al. [43], who used the FDEMATEL-ANP-DEA 
approach for petroleum companies in Iran; Jafari Nodeh (2020), who 
utilized MLP-DT-K-means for SS; Cheng et al. [17] and Amindoust [1], 
who applied hybrid models including FIS, AR, AdaBoost, and DEA; and 
studies by Aggarwal et al. [5], Zhu et al. [66], and Abdulla et al. [3], 
which integrated DEA with SVM, DT, ISVM, and BPNN.
This study proposes an intelligent hybrid method that combines the 
MPSS-DEA approach with MLP, CHAID, and KNN algorithms. Initially, 
the MPSS-DEA model is used to identify the most productive suppliers 
and classify observations into MPSS and non-MPSS groups. Subse­
quently, supplier performance is evaluated, and the most influential 
criteria are extracted using MLP, CHAID, and KNN.
The proposed method offers several advantages:
1. Reduced algorithm runtime when adding new observations 
compared to standalone MPSS-DEA;
2. Fast and timely replacement of underperforming suppliers with 
similar alternatives;
3. Grouping suppliers into comparable categories for quicker and 
more efficient selection.
3. Proposed methodology
This section presents a novel intelligent algorithm for SSS. Before 
discussing the main topic, it should be noted that the three terms- 
criteria, input and output parameters, and features- refer to the same 
concept. Each term is used according to its corresponding model: criteria 
(suppliers’ questionnaires), input and output parameters (MPSS-DEA 
model), and features (ANNs, CHAID, and K-NN algorithms). Fig. 3 il­
lustrates the main structure of the proposed hybrid intelligent algorithm.
The algorithm, shown in Fig. 3, comprises three phases. In the first 
phase, the collected datasets undergo cleansing using one of the studied 
preprocessing methods for dimensionality reduction. This process 
eliminates missing and outlier data while selecting relevant, unique, and 
monotonic features. All features are examined to determine their types 
and relationships, including both linear and nonlinear connections. The 
selected datasets are then imported into the MPSS-DEA model to eval­
uate supplier performance.
Efficiency rates are used to gauge supplier sustainability: an effi­
ciency rate (eo) of one indicates sustainability, while eo < 1 indicates 
unsustainability. Based on these results, suppliers are categorized into 
sustainable and unsustainable groups, which are then split into training 
and testing datasets- typically in an 80/20 or 70/30 ratio- with the 
smaller portion reserved for testing and the larger for training the ML 
model. Upon completing this process, the second phase begins, where 
training datasets are imported into the selected ML models for learning. 
In this phase, each hybrid algorithm- DEANN, DEA-CHAID, and DEA- 
KNN- follows specific steps within SPSS Modeler version 18.0.
In phase three, confusion matrices, predictor importance charts, and 
ROC curves are extracted for each hybrid algorithm. The confusion 
matrix for the training dataset is generated using key metrics such as 
accuracy, precision, and F1-score. Additionally, the weights of features 
important for evaluating supplier sustainability are identified and 
prioritized within each hybrid algorithm. The ROC curves illustrate the 
M. GholamAzad and A. Eydi                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Ain Shams Engineering Journal 17 (2026) 103871 
6
10:
strengths to the hybrid framework. Phases II and III were executed using 
SPSS Modeler software version 18.0. Appendix B presents the general 
schematic of the software environment used. The following section 
provides a summary of each algorithm and the results obtained from the 
hybrid MPSS-AI approach.
4.2.1. Mlp-anns algorithm
The Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) is a feedforward neural network 
and represents the simplest form of ANNs, consisting of multiple inter­
connected perceptron models. The input layer receives external data and 
transmits it to one or more hidden layers, which are responsible for 
learning complex patterns within the dataset[39]. These hidden layers 
enable the model to perform predictions or classifications. Outputs are 
generated through activation functions applied to the processed signals 
from previous layers. These functions are nonlinear and selected based 
on the nature of the data. All layers in the MLP are fully connected, and 
the use of a loss function helps mitigate overfitting during training [55]. 
In this study, the hyperbolic tangent (TanH) function is employed as the 
activation function. TanH is a nonlinear function centered at zero, with a 
range between −1 and 1. It is commonly used in hidden layers due to its 
zero-centered mean, which facilitates data normalization and enhances 
learning in subsequent layers. The TanH activation function is mathe­
matically defined in Equation (5) [31]. 
tanhx = sinhx
coshx = ex −e−x
ex + e−x = e2x −1
e2x + 1
(5) 
Various activation functions can be employed in the hidden layers of 
neural networks, including ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit), Leaky ReLU, 
Sigmoid, TanH, and Softmax. Among these, TanH is often preferred for 
hidden layers due to its output range of −1 to 1, which facilitates faster 
convergence and improved learning dynamics compared to other func­
tions. The key advantages of MLP-based ANNs include their flexibility, 
computational strength, and ease of implementation. MLPs are widely 
used across diverse domains such as regression analysis, classification 
tasks, time series forecasting, and image recognition. Their ability to 
learn and generalize complex patterns within data enables highly ac­
curate predictions and classifications [49].
4.2.2. DEANN
Step 10: Implementing the MLP Model to Determine Neuron 
Count and Outputs: Fig. 6 presents the model summary and configu­
ration of the DEANN algorithm following dataset import. The summary 
includes key details such as the target variable, neural network type, 
stopping rule (for multilayer perceptron), and the number of neurons in 
each hidden layer. SPSS Modeler automatically determined the optimal 
architecture, resulting in a single hidden layer with three neurons- 
selected based on data complexity and supplier record volume. The 
dataset was partitioned into a model-building set and an overfit- 
prevention set, with 30% allocated to the latter. The DEANN model 
executed in 15 s across multiple random seeds, consistently achieving an 
accuracy rate of 91.9%. This confirms the suitability of the selected 
architecture and activation functions for the classification task.
Step 11: Determining Activation Functions for Hidden and 
Output Layers: In this study, the TanH activation function was applied 
to the hidden layers, while the Softmax function was used for the output 
layer. The Softmax activation function transforms the raw outputs of the 
neural network into a probability distribution across the input classes, 
making it particularly suitable for classification tasks. It is commonly 
employed in the final layer of classification networks due to its inter­
pretability as a probability distribution. To compute Softmax, a vector z 
containing K real-valued elements is used as input. Each element in z is 
exponentiated using the mathematical constant e ≈2.718, ensuring all 
values are positive. These exponentiated values are then normalized by 
dividing each by the sum of all exponentiated elements, resulting in a 
new vector of the same size where each element represents a probability 
between 0 and 1. The class prediction is typically made using the argmax 
function, which identifies the index of the highest probability. The 
Softmax function is mathematically defined as follows Shatravin et al. 
[52]: 
softmax(zi) =
exp(zi)
∑
jexp(zi)
(6) 
The output layer of a neural network can utilize various activation 
functions depending on the nature of the task. For binary classification, 
the Sigmoid function is commonly used. In multi-class classification 
problems, the Softmax function is preferred due to its ability to convert 
raw outputs into a normalized probability distribution across multiple 
classes. For regression tasks, a Linear activation function is typically 
applied, while multi-label classification often employs a Sigmoid func­
tion for each output node. In this study, the Softmax function was 
selected for the output layer to ensure that the model produces a prob­
ability distribution where the sum of all class probabilities equals 1. This 
normalization facilitates accurate interpretation and reliable class pre­
diction.
Step 12. Training the model and calculate the MLP weight vec­
tor: Fig. 7 illustrates the predictor importance chart generated by the 
DEANN algorithm after training the model using 70% of the dataset. The 
structure of the underlying MLP network is provided in Appendix C.
The primary objective of employing the ANN model in this context is

set of predictors is used. This approach helps identify which features

The chart displays the relative importance of each feature on a scale 
from 0.0 to 1.0, with all values normalized to sum to 1.0. The most

CA6, SE4, SC4, SC2, CS4, CS1, CA2, SE5, and DE3.
Although the importance scores are relatively close- reflecting the

proximity relationships among suppliers in the multidimensional 
feature space.
The chart also confirms that the DEA-KNN algorithm distributes

fication is influenced by a combination of operational, compliance, and 
quality-related criteria. This balanced weighting supports the model’s 
robustness and generalizability across different supplier profiles.
4.3. Phase III
Step 13: Prediction and Outcome Assessment: In this phase, 
predictions are generated using the trained AI models. The accuracy and 
reliability of these predictions are evaluated using performance metrics 
such as the confusion matrix and feature importance weights. The 
confusion matrix provides insight into classification accuracy by de­
tailing true positives (TP), false positives (FP), true negatives (TN), and 
false negatives (FN). Feature weights help identify the most influential 
variables contributing to the model’s decisions. If the results are deemed 
trustworthy and satisfactory- demonstrating high predictive accuracy 
and consistent feature relevance- the optimal hybrid approach is 
selected for final implementation. However, if the outcomes fall short of 
expectations, the process reverts to Phase II for reassessment and 
refinement of model parameters, data preparation, or algorithm 
configuration.
Step 14: Generating the Confusion Matrix for Model Compari­
son: In this step, the performance of each classification model is eval­
uated using a confusion matrix. This matrix offers a concise summary of 
correct and incorrect predictions, organized by class, and is commonly 
referred to as an error matrix due to its role in visualizing model mis­
classifications. It compares predicted values against actual outcomes, 
providing insight into the model’s accuracy and error distribution across 
categories. The confusion matrix includes the following key components 
[34]: 
• TP: The number of records correctly classified as efficient by the 
model.
• TN: The number of records correctly classified as inefficient.
• FP: Instances where inefficient suppliers were incorrectly predicted 
as efficient (Type I error).
• FN: Instances where efficient suppliers were incorrectly predicted as 
inefficient (Type II error).
Table 9 presents the confusion matrix results for the training dataset 
across three hybrid algorithms: DEANN-DEA, DEA-CHAID, and DEA- 
KNN.
Calculations Using the Confusion Matrix: The confusion matrix 
serves as the foundation for evaluating classification model performance 
through several key metrics. These include classification accuracy, 
precision, recall, and F −measure, each offering distinct insights into the 
model’s predictive behavior [27].
1 Classification Accuracy: Accuracy measures the proportion of 
correct predictions made by the model out of all predictions. It is 
calculated using the formula: 
Accuracy =
TP + TN
TP + FP + FN + TN
(9)

calculated separately for each class. The formulas are as follows [27]:

4F-Measure: The F-measure, or F1 −
score, is a harmonic mean of 
precision and recall, designed to provide a balanced evaluation when 
these two metrics diverge. This is particularly useful when one model 
exhibits high precision but low recall, or vice versa, making direct 
comparison difficult. The F-measure reaches its maximum value when 
precision and recall are equal, offering a unified metric for classification 
performance. The formulas are as follows [27]: 
• Positive Class F-Measure
F −Measure+ = 2.Recall+.Precision+
Recall+ + Precision+

The F-measure rates for the DEANN model are 51.47 % for positive and 
95.44 % for negative.
These results indicate that the DEANN model performs exceptionally 
well in identifying inefficient suppliers (negative class), while its per­
formance in detecting efficient ones (positive class) is moderate.
5 Misclassification Rate: The misclassification rate, also referred to 
as the error rate, quantifies how frequently the model produces incorrect 
predictions. It is a critical metric for evaluating the reliability of classi­
fication models, especially when comparing multiple algorithms. The 
error rate is calculated as the ratio of incorrect predictions to the total 
number of predictions, using the following formula: 
Errorrate =
FP + FN
TP + FP + FN + TN
(13) 
This metric complements accuracy by highlighting the proportion of 
errors, thereby offering a more complete view of model performance.
Table 10 presents the misclassification rates for the three hybrid 
algorithms individually. These rates were derived from the confusion 
matrix results shown in Table 9.
As shown, the DEA-CHAID model achieved the highest accuracy 
(92.5 %), followed by DEA-ANN (91.9 %) and DEA-KNN (91.4 %). The 
misclassification rates are relatively low across all models, indicating 
their strong ability to differentiate between efficient and inefficient 
suppliers based on the selected sustainability features. These results 
confirm the reliability and robustness of the proposed hybrid framework 
and further justify its applicability in large-scale supplier evaluation 
tasks.
Determining optimal hyper-parameters is a critical step in building 
reliable ML models, as these parameters directly influence classification 
accuracy, generalization, and computational efficiency. In the context of 
DEA-ML frameworks, the choice of hyper-parameter optimization 
technique depends on several factors, including model complexity, 
available computational resources, and the specific goals of the analysis. 
While advanced strategies such as random search, grid search, or 
Bayesian optimization can be employed to fine-tune model perfor­
mance, they often require significant computational effort. To balance 
efficiency and reliability, this study adopts cross-validation as an eval­
uation strategy. Although cross-validation is not a direct optimization 
method, it plays a vital role in assessing how well the selected hyper- 
parameters generalize to unseen data. By partitioning the dataset into 
training and testing folds, cross-validation ensures that the model avoids 
overfitting and maintains consistent performance across different sub­
sets of the data. This approach is particularly suitable for high- 
dimensional supplier datasets, where computational cost and model 
interpretability must be carefully managed [27,63]. For example, Fig. 15
illustrates the ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve for the 
DEA-CHAID model, comparing its performance on both training and 
testing datasets. The ROC curve is a widely accepted diagnostic tool for 
evaluating the predictive capability of classification models. It plots the 
True Positive Rate (Sensitivity) against the False Positive Rate (1 −
Specificity) across various decision thresholds. Each point on the curve 
represents a sensitivity/specificity pair corresponding to a specific cut- 
off value. A model with perfect discrimination will have a curve that 
passes through the upper-left corner of the plot (100 % sensitivity, 0 % 
false positive rate). Therefore, the closer the ROC curve is to this corner, 
the better the model’s overall classification performance. In the training 
partition, the DEA-CHAID model achieved: (Sensitivity: 0.9688, and 
False Positive Rate (1 −Specificity): 0.1916); in the testing partition, the 
model yielded: (Sensitivity: 0.75; False Positive Rate (1 −
Specificity): 
0.125).
These results indicate that the model performs exceptionally well on 
the training data, with high sensitivity and acceptable false positive 
rates. On the testing data, while sensitivity slightly decreases, the false 
positive rate also improves, suggesting that the model generalizes 
reasonably well to unseen data. The ROC curve confirms the robustness 
of the DEA-CHAID algorithm in distinguishing between efficient and 
inefficient suppliers. It also validates the effectiveness of the selected 
features and decision tree structure in capturing meaningful patterns 
within the dataset. Additionally, while this study did not employ 
advanced hyper-parameter optimization techniques such as Bayesian 
optimization or grid search, cross-validation was used to ensure that the 
selected parameters generalize effectively. This approach balances 
computational efficiency with model reliability, especially in high- 
dimensional, real-world industrial datasets.
Fig. 16 presents the ROC curves for the three hybrid algorithms: 
DEA-CHAID, DEA-KNN, and DEANN, evaluated on both training and 
testing datasets.
As shown in Fig. 16: The DEA-CHAID and DEA-KNN models exhibit 
similar ROC patterns, with moderate separation between classes and 
consistent performance across both partitions.
The DEANN model demonstrates a noticeably stronger ROC curve, 
with higher sensitivity and lower false positive rates in both training and 
testing sets. This indicates that the DEANN model achieves better class 
separation and generalization, outperforming the other two hybrid 
models in terms of predictive accuracy and robustness. The superior 
curve shape of DEANN suggests that it is more effective in identifying 
efficient suppliers (MPSS) while minimizing misclassification of ineffi­
cient ones.
Overall, the ROC analysis confirms that while all three models are 
reliable, DEANN offers the highest classification performance, making it 
the most suitable choice for sustainable supplier selection in this study.
Step 15: Calculating Feature Weights and Efficiency Scores for 
Each Supplier: In this stage, feature weights and efficiency scores are 
computed for each supplier using the corresponding hybrid algorithms. 
These weights reflect the relative importance of each input variable in 
influencing the model’s predictions and are derived from the internal

according to the core criteria of the P petrochemical industry.
Step 16. Stop.
5. Discussion
Selecting the right supplier is a major challenge for SCMs across 
various industries, especially in the era of big data. The process of 
identifying suitable suppliers and replacing underperforming ones is 
often time-consuming and prone to errors. This study introduces a 
robust hybrid framework that integrates the MPSS-DEA with three 
intelligent algorithms- ANN, CHAID, and KNN −
to address these limi­
tations. A comparative review of prior studies highlights the novelty of 
this approach. While Bafrooei et al. [11], Wongthong and Paoprasert 
[60], and Nasri et al. [43] focused on static criteria or limited datasets, 
our model dynamically evaluates supplier efficiency using real-time 
data inputs and handles high-dimensional environments. Jafari Nodeh 
et al. [30] and Cheng et al. [17] applied ML techniques, but their 
model’s lacked integration with DEA’s scale efficiency logic, which is 
central to our MPSS-based evaluation. The proposed hybrid algorithms 
calculate supplier efficiency scores based on the MPSS framework, 
enabling accurate classification of efficient and inefficient suppliers. 
Table 13 presents the efficiency scores (eo) of selected DMUs across 
three hybrid models: DEANN, DEA-CHAID, and DEA-KNN. These scores 
are derived from the MPSS-based evaluation process and reflect each 
supplier’s relative performance within the dataset.

DEA-CHAID consistently assigns high efficiency scores, with many 
DMUs receiving values close to 0.99. This indicates a strong tendency 
toward optimistic classification, potentially favoring suppliers with 
borderline performance.
DEANN demonstrates a more balanced distribution, with efficiency 
scores ranging from 0.76 to 0.99. This suggests that the neural network 
model captures nuanced patterns and avoids overfitting, offering a more 
realistic assessment of supplier performance.
DEA-KNN shows moderate scores, with several DMUs receiving 
values around 0.80 and some as low as 0.60. This reflects the algorithm’s 
sensitivity to local data structures and its reliance on neighborhood 
similarity, which may lead to conservative classification in sparse 
regions.
For instance, S155, identified as an MPSS supplier in the DEANN 
model, receives lower scores in DEA-CHAID (0.71) and DEA-KNN (0.80), 
highlighting the neural network’s ability to detect scale-efficient sup­
pliers more accurately. Similarly, DMU S156 shows a significant varia­
tion across models- 0.96 in DEANN, 0.56 in DEA-CHAID, and 0.80 in 
DEA-KNN- indicating that DEA-CHAID may underestimate certain sup­
pliers due to its tree-based structure.
Overall, the efficiency scores across the three models are closely 
aligned, but DEANN offers a more refined and discriminative evaluation, 
especially for borderline or complex cases. These results validate the 
robustness of the proposed hybrid approach and its applicability in 
large-scale supplier selection tasks.
As shown in Table 13, the efficiency scores across DEANN, DEA- 
CHAID, and DEA-KNN are closely aligned, with classification accu­
racies of 91.9%, 92.5%, and 91.4%, respectively. These results confirm 
the reliability and consistency of the model.
5.1. Practical applications and strategic value
This study presents various practical applications and strategic 
values such as:
1. Real-Time Supplier Replacement: Organizations can swiftly 
identify and substitute underperforming suppliers, minimizing opera­
tional disruptions and improving SC resilience.
2. Criteria Prioritization: Key features such as CA6, DE3, QA3, CO2, 
and QA1 consistently emerge across all models, guiding procurement 
teams in refining audit checklists and contract terms.
3. Integration with ERP Systems: The algorithms can be embedded 
into enterprise resource planning (ERP) platforms, enabling automated 
scoring, dashboard visualization, and continuous monitoring.
4. Robustness to Market Dynamics: The model supports dynamic 
adjustment based on new data inputs, supplier turnover, and regulatory 
changes, ensuring long-term relevance and adaptability.
5. The robustness of the proposed intelligent method is further 
validated through adaptability to new data, model flexibility, sensitivity 
analysis, scenario planning, and stakeholder engagement. The system’s 
ability to process real-time inputs and adjust parameters based on 
evolving trends ensures sustained performance across various opera­
tional scenarios.
In summary, this study contributes a scalable, interpretable, and 
data-driven solution for SSS. By combining DEA’s mathematical rigor 
with AI’s predictive power, the framework bridges the gap between 
theoretical efficiency analysis and practical decision-making in 
competitive supply chains.
Despite the strong performance of the proposed hybrid framework, a 
few limitations are worth noting to guide future research. First, the 
model was applied within the petrochemical industry, which may affect 
its generalizability to other sectors with distinct sustainability dynamics. 
However, the framework’s modular design allows for adaptation to 
other domains with appropriate data calibration. Second, like all AI- 
based systems, model accuracy is sensitive to data quality and bal­
ance; future studies may benefit from larger and more diverse datasets to 
further enhance robustness. Third, while DEA-AI integration improves 
predictive power, interpretability remains a challenge- particularly for 
ANN- highlighting the need for explainable AI approaches to strengthen 
managerial trust. Additionally, although the framework supports real- 
time classification, the initial DEA evaluation relies on static data 
snapshots; incorporating dynamic data streams could improve respon­
siveness to changing supplier conditions. Finally, the computational 
demands of training multiple AI models may pose constraints for smaller 
organizations, suggesting the value of scalable or cloud-based deploy­
ment strategies.
6. Conclusion
6.1. Findings
This study presents a novel and intelligent integrated framework for 
SSS by merging the mathematical MPSS-DEA model with three AI al­
gorithms: ANN, CHAID, and KNN. The proposed hybrid method was 
implemented at P Petrochemical in Iran, involving 362 suppliers and 38 
sustainability criteria across economic, environmental, and social 
dimensions.
The framework alleviates the computational complexity typically 
associated with traditional DEA models, especially in big data contexts. 
By integrating AI algorithms, the model significantly reduces runtime 
and enables accurate classification of suppliers into efficient (MPSS) and 
inefficient (non-MPSS) categories. This intelligent strategy also supports 
rapid substitution of underperforming suppliers with suitable alterna­
tives, enhancing supply chain resilience.
The algorithms used in this study are both visual and interpretable, 
allowing for effective ranking and weighting of primary criteria. The 
results demonstrated high classification accuracy across all models-DEA- 
CHAID (92.5%), DEA-ANN (91.9%), and DEA-KNN (91.4%)- confirming 
the reliability and robustness of the proposed method for application in 
similar industrial settings.
To further evaluate model performance, Receiver Operating Char­
acteristic (ROC) curves were generated for each hybrid algorithm. The 
Area under the Curve (AUC) values were as follows: 
• DEA-CHAID: AUC = 0.96
• DEA-ANN: AUC =0.95
• DEA-KNN: AUC =0.94
These results indicate excellent discrimination ability across all 
models, with DEA-CHAID again outperforming the others. The ROC 
analysis confirms that the proposed framework can effectively distin­
guish between efficient and inefficient suppliers, even in complex, high- 
dimensional datasets.
Beyond computational efficiency, the hybrid DEA-ML framework 
provides long-term strategic benefits in terms of sustainability and 
ethics. It enables organizations to evaluate suppliers not only on tradi­
tional metrics such as cost, quality, and delivery, but also on sustain­
ability indicators like environmental impact and social responsibility. 
This comprehensive evaluation supports benchmarking, encourages 
suppliers to improve their practices, and promotes greener SCs.
The model also facilitates lifecycle analysis, allowing firms to assess 
the environmental footprint of suppliers throughout product lifecycles. 
By leveraging advanced analytics, the framework increases trans­
parency and accountability in SS, particularly regarding ethical prac­
tices such as labor conditions and fair trade. Embedding these values 
into the selection process aligns with modern consumer expectations 
and regulatory standards, fostering responsible and future-oriented SCs.
A key challenge addressed in this study is the adaptability of the 
MPSS-ML method across industries with varying data characteristics and 
operational complexities. The authors demonstrate that selecting 
appropriate input/output variables and applying proper normalization 
techniques allows meaningful comparisons across diverse datasets. The 
modular 
design 
of 
the 
framework 
supports 
scalability 
and

customization, enabling components to be added or modified without 
overhauling the entire system. This flexibility ensures that the DEA-ML 
approach remains effective across different industrial contexts, pro­
moting continuous learning and tailored decision-making.
In summary, the proposed hybrid framework not only enhances 
supplier evaluation accuracy and efficiency but also contributes to sus­
tainable development, ethical sourcing, and strategic supply chain 
management.
6.2. Research limitations
There were limitations to using the proposed algorithms, such as:
1 The framework was tested only in the petrochemical industry, 
which may limit its generalizability to other sectors.
2. AI algorithms require large, high-quality datasets; however, data 
availability is restricted in some industries due to confidentiality. In our 
case, company names and sensitive details could not be disclosed.
3. The MPSS-DEA model is not suitable for uncertain or fuzzy envi­
ronments; fuzzy-based models may be more appropriate in such 
contexts.
4. Some AI models, such as ANN, lack interpretability, which may 
reduce managerial trust in automated decisions.
5. The initial DEA evaluation is based on static data snapshots and 
may not reflect dynamic supplier conditions.
6. Training multiple AI models on large datasets demands significant 
computational resources, which may be challenging for smaller 
organizations.
6.3. Recommendations for future research
Future studies may consider the following directions to enhance and 
expand the proposed hybrid framework:
1. Cross-Industry Adaptation: Extend the framework to other in­
dustries with varying sustainability priorities and operational com­
plexities to test its generalizability and robustness
2. Uncertainty Modeling: Incorporate fuzzy logic or probabilistic 
models to better handle uncertainty and vagueness in supplier perfor­
mance data, especially in environments with incomplete or imprecise 
information.
3. Model Interpretability: Enhance the transparency and interpret­
ability of AI components to support managerial decision-making and 
increase trust in automated evaluations.
4. Dynamic DEA Modeling: Develop adaptive DEA models capable of 
updating efficiency scores in real time as new suppliers or data become 
available, enabling continuous evaluation.
5. Scalable Computing: Explore cloud-based or distributed 
computing solutions to reduce the computational cost of training and 
deploying large-scale hybrid models.
6. Real-Time DSS Integration: Integrate the framework into real-time 
decision support systems (DSS) for continuous supplier monitoring, risk 
mitigation, and strategic planning.
In future research, the authors plan to apply the proposed intelligent 
methodology to comparable sectors and adapt it into a unified appli­
cation for use across all petrochemical industries. Emphasis will be 
placed on flexibility and environmental impact when assessing and 
selecting suppliers in critical sectors. The long-term goal is to develop a 
comprehensive and scalable approach that combines deep neural net­
works with metaheuristic optimization techniques to enhance supplier 
selection across diverse supply chains.

Appendix C 
Fig. 19 illustrates the architecture of the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) network used in the DEANN model. This structure represents a feedforward 
neural network with a single hidden layer comprising three neurons. Each input node—representing a supplier evaluation criterion—is fully con­
nected to all neurons in the hidden layer, ensuring that the model captures complex nonlinear relationships among features.
The input layer includes key predictors such as CA6, CS2, DE5, DE3, DE2, QA3, CS4, CO1, DE1, CS3, R4, and CO2, along with a bias term to 
enhance learning flexibility. These inputs are processed through the hidden neurons using the TanH activation function, which enables the model to 
learn centered and normalized representations. The outputs from the hidden layer are then passed to the final node labeled MPSS, which represents the 
classification outcome—efficient or inefficient supplier.
This architecture was automatically optimized using SPSS Modeler, resulting in a compact yet powerful structure capable of achieving high 
classification accuracy (91.9 %) while maintaining computational efficiency. The fully connected design ensures that all relevant features contribute 
to the final decision, and the use of TanH and Softmax activation functions supports both convergence and interpretability.
